name: Austria
summary: Strong IT demand, cheap English MSc, excellent healthcare and top-tier safety, but a ~10-year citizenship with a near-total dual-citizenship ban, German B1→B2 bars, in-person New Delhi processing for Bangladeshis, high tax and a tightening migration climate make it a hard fit for a dual-preferring Bangladeshi couple.

job-market.summary: Software/data engineers on the 2026 nationwide shortage list (64 occupations); deep DACH tech demand, strong pay, both placeable.
job-market.reasoning: The Fachkräfteverordnung 2026 (BGBl II 316/2025) lists 64 nationwide shortage occupations, with categories 21 and 34 covering 70+ data-processing/software-engineer titles, so both engineers fit shortage routes that waive the AMS labour-market test. Austria reports a deficit of ~24,000 IT specialists and Vienna dev pay runs ~€58k average to ~€86k senior (2026); English-only roles cluster in Vienna while German widens options, capping this just below the top band.
evidence: 2026 nationwide shortage list = 64 occupations; categories 21 (engineers, data processing) + 34 (graduate engineers, data processing) include software developers, software engineers, system analysts, IT architects.
Shortage status waives the AMS labour-market test and lowers RWR points/salary bars (Fachkräfteverordnung 2026, eff. Jan 2026).
~24,000 IT specialists lacking nationally; SalaryExpert 2026 Vienna software developer avg ~€86k, Glassdoor avg ~€58k.
Both spouses are skilled CS graduates, so both can target shortage-occupation employment.

visa-access.summary: Residence permits, work visa AND job-seeker visa need in-person appearance at the Embassy New Delhi; only D-visa lodging is in Dhaka.
visa-access.reasoning: Per the Austrian Embassy New Delhi, applications for residence permits (over 6 months), work visas and the job-seeker visa must be made in person in New Delhi by Bangladeshi applicants; only D-visa lodging and biometrics run through the VFS centre in Gulshan, Dhaka, and decisions are made by New Delhi. The student MSc residence permit therefore likely also requires Delhi travel, plus VFS fees and subsistence/health-insurance proof, which is real friction versus countries that decide fully in Dhaka.
evidence: BMEIA New Delhi (2026): residence permits >6 months, work visas and job-seeker visas must be applied for in person at the Austrian Embassy New Delhi.
Schengen C-visas handled by the French Embassy in Dhaka; long-stay D-visas lodged at VFS Delta Life Tower, Gulshan, Dhaka.
All applications assessed by the Austrian Embassy New Delhi; fingerprints required if none in last 59 months.
VFS service/processing fees apply on top of the embassy fee.

links.title: BMEIA – Austrian Embassy New Delhi: how to apply
VFS Global – Austria visa from Bangladesh

citizenship.summary: ~10 years residency, near-total dual-citizenship ban (renounce in 2 yrs), German B1 rising to B2. Deal-breaker for a dual-preferring couple.
citizenship.reasoning: Standard naturalisation requires 10 years' residence (incl. 5 with a settlement permit), and the official oesterreich.gv.at page confirms naturalised citizens must give up prior citizenship (renounce within 2 years) save narrow 'special interest' exceptions, so both would lose Bangladeshi nationality. The 2025-2029 Stocker coalition plans to raise required German from B1 to B2 for standard naturalisation; only a 6-year fast track exists, itself needing B2. For a couple who explicitly prefer dual citizenship this is near-disqualifying.
evidence: Standard naturalisation after 10 years (incl. 5 with settlement permit); 6-year route requires B2 German + integration proof.
oesterreich.gv.at: applicants must give up previous citizenship; renounce within 2 years if not automatic; dual allowed only in narrow exceptions.
Coalition programme plans B1→B2 German for citizenship; reform expected 2026 (The Local, 10 Dec 2025).
Foreign residents call the renunciation rule the decisive reason they will not apply (The Local, 8 Jan 2026).

links.title: The Local – Austria's strict dual-citizenship rules

post-study-work.summary: 12-month job-seeker extension after graduation, then RWR Card for graduates with no points and no labour-market test.
post-study-work.reasoning: Graduates of Austrian higher-education institutions can renew the student permit for 12 months to job-hunt, then switch to a Red-White-Red Card for graduates on a qualification-matched job at the locally customary graduate salary, with no points system and no AMS labour-market test (migration.gv.at, 2026). The RWR Card then carries full labour-market access so the spouse can move to an RWR Card plus and work freely. It is a clear, predictable route, but conditional on landing a qualifying offer and shorter than the 2-3 year open-work permits in Canada/NZ.
evidence: Student permit renewable for 12 months post-graduation to seek work or start a business.
RWR Card for graduates: qualification-matched contract at locally customary graduate junior salary; no points, no labour-market test.
2026 RWR Card salary floor €3,465/month gross; graduate route benchmarked to comparable Austrian junior pay.
RWR Card grants full labour-market access, enabling the spouse's RWR Card plus.

links.title: VisaHQ – 2026 RWR / Blue Card salary thresholds

spouse-family.summary: Work-phase spouse works freely (RWR-Plus); study-phase spouse needs an AMS permit + labour-market check; 2026 reunion quotas add risk.
spouse-family.reasoning: In the work phase the dependant gets a Red-White-Red Card plus with free labour-market access, but during the MSc phase the accompanying spouse holds a 12-month family-reunification stay permit and can work only after an employer obtains an AMS Beschäftigungsbewilligung subject to a labour-market check. A 2025 law (eff. into 2026) also lets the government pause family reunification by ordinance until 30 Sep 2026 and introduces a capacity-based quota model, adding real uncertainty for a couple who must relocate together and both want to work from day one.
evidence: RWR Card plus grants free labour-market access to family members of card holders (work phase).
Student-permit dependants get a 12-month 'Stay Permit – Family Reunification' and need an AMS work permit after a labour-market check before working (study phase).
Spouse must be 21+ at application; permit issued for max 12 months at a time.
2025 law permits ordinance-based pause of family reunion until 30 Sep 2026, then a quota model from 2026 (The Local, 21 Dec 2025).

links.title: The Local – Immigration changes in Austria 2026 and beyond

msc-study.summary: 20+ English CS MSc, very low public tuition (~€726.72/semester), 20h/week student work; but study-phase spouse access is restricted.
msc-study.reasoning: Austrian public universities charge non-EU students only €726.72/semester (~€1,453/yr) plus a €26.20 ÖH fee, with ~20 English-taught CS Master's across 15 institutions and OeAD scholarships available. Non-EU Master's students may work 20h/week during term (full-time in breaks) once an employer secures an AMS permit. The main drag for this couple is the restricted study-phase work for the accompanying spouse, keeping it out of the top band.
evidence: Non-EU public-university tuition €726.72/semester (~€1,453/yr) + €26.20 ÖH fee — far below UK/US/Australia.
Mastersportal lists ~20 English-taught Master's in Computer Sciences across 15 universities (2026).
Master's students may work 20h/week in term, full-time in lecture-free months (AMS Beschäftigungsbewilligung still required).
OeAD scholarships and central study portal support international applicants.

links.title: Mastersportal – Computer Science & IT Master's in Austria

pr-pathway.summary: Long-term Resident – EU after 5 years, but needs B1 German (Module 2), income bar and integration; settlement quotas add friction.
pr-pathway.reasoning: Daueraufenthalt-EU is reachable after 5 years of continuous lawful residence, which is solid, but it requires Integration Agreement Module 2 (B1 German), proof of stable income (~€1,274/month single, 2025) and a clean record. Student years count only partially toward the clock and Austria's settlement system runs on annual quotas plus, from 2026, capacity-based limits, so timing to PR is not fully predictable for this couple.
evidence: Long-term Resident – EU permit after 5 years' continuous lawful residence (limited permitted absences).
Requires Integration Agreement Module 2 (B1 German) and proof of regular income.
Study-permit years count only partially toward the 5-year clock; settlement is quota-bound.
2026 introduces capacity-based migration limits, adding unpredictability.

income-cost.summary: Good gross dev pay (~€58-86k Vienna) but ~18% employee social load, top 55% tax and ~€1,300 rents compress net savings to moderate.
income-cost.reasoning: Two software salaries (Vienna dev ~€58k avg to ~€86k senior, 2026) give a solid gross, but ~18% employee social contributions plus progressive income tax (top 55%) and Vienna one-bed rents averaging ~€1,300/month leave moderate, not strong, savings. The 14-pay system (bonus months taxed at 6%), a €365 annual transit pass and rent regulation help, but on two high incomes the tax-and-rent drag keeps this mid-band.
evidence: Vienna software developer pay ~€58k (Glassdoor) to ~€86k senior (SalaryExpert), 2026.
Employee social contributions ~18% of gross; income tax progressive to 55%; 13th/14th salary taxed at flat 6%.
Vienna one-bed rent averages ~€1,300/month (€730–€1,700 range); single living cost ~€2,000+/month.
Annual public-transport pass €365; rent regulation limits increases.

links.title: Numbeo – Cost of Living in Vienna (Jun 2026)

healthcare.summary: Near-universal statutory insurance (99.9% covered), high quality, life expectancy 81.3, out-of-pocket under ~15%.
healthcare.reasoning: Austria's social health insurance covers ~99.9% of residents with comprehensive financial protection and out-of-pocket spending under ~15% of total health expenditure (WHO Euro Observatory). Life expectancy is 81.3 years, above the OECD average, with high physician/bed density and no formal gatekeeping. Both spouses are covered cheaply via employment or student insurance, with some non-emergency specialist wait times the only notable gap.
evidence: Near-universal coverage; >99% of population insured through SHI funds (WHO, 2023 data).
Out-of-pocket spending under ~15% of total health spending; cost-sharing exemptions for vulnerable groups.
Life expectancy at birth 81.3 years, above OECD average; high physician and hospital-bed density.
Direct access to GPs, specialists and hospitals (no gatekeeping); some non-emergency specialist waits.

culture-language.summary: English works in tech and study, but B1 German gates PR, rising-B2 German gates citizenship, and German is needed for officialdom.
culture-language.reasoning: Vienna tech work and English-taught study run fine in English, but German is unavoidable for administration, schooling and public services, and is a hard gate for PR (B1, Integration Agreement Module 2) and citizenship (B1 rising to B2 under the 2025-2029 coalition). Livability is high, yet for a couple targeting long-term settlement the German requirement is a real structural barrier.
evidence: Integration Agreement Module 2 (B1 German) required for Long-term Resident – EU permit.
Citizenship German requirement set to rise from B1 to B2 (coalition plan, The Local 10 Dec 2025).
English common in Vienna tech roles, but German needed for administration, schooling and public services.
New Jan 2026 integration courses (5-day values/orientation) add German-context obligations.

links.title: The Local – Immigration changes in Austria 2026 and beyond

safety-law.summary: Top-tier rule of law (WJP 2025 top ~12-15 of 143) and very low crime; stable institutions, though migrant-policy climate is hardening.
safety-law.reasoning: Austria sits in the top ~12-15 of 143 countries in the WJP Rule of Law Index 2025 (which it improved on, against a global decline of 68% of countries), with low crime and reliable courts, giving the couple strong day-to-day security and legal protection. The minor deduction reflects a hardening migration-enforcement climate (new integration sanctions, family-reunion pause powers) rather than any breakdown in personal safety or the justice system.
evidence: WJP Rule of Law Index 2025 assessed 143 countries (Nordics + NZ top 5); Austria consistently top ~12-15 and improved in 2025.
Global rule-of-law decline: 68% of countries fell in 2025; Austria was among the minority that improved.
Very low violent crime and safe public spaces; stable, well-functioning institutions and courts.
Hardening enforcement climate (integration sanctions, family-reunion pause powers) is the only downward pull.

links.title: WJP Rule of Law Index 2025 – global press release
WJP Rule of Law Index – Austria

politics.summary: Centrist ÖVP–SPÖ–NEOS coalition governs since Mar 2025, but FPÖ won the 2024 vote and is the leading opposition; migration rules tightening.
politics.reasoning: The FPÖ topped the Sept 2024 election (28.8%) on a 'remigration' platform, but a centrist three-party Stocker coalition (ÖVP–SPÖ–NEOS) took office on 3 Mar 2025 and the far right sits in opposition, so governance is stable in the near term. However, that coalition itself is tightening migration: stricter asylum, B1→B2 German for citizenship, integration sanctions and family-reunion pause/quota powers, and the FPÖ leads many polls, so policy volatility and an anti-immigrant trend are real risks for Muslim Bangladeshi newcomers.
evidence: FPÖ won the Sept 2024 election with 28.8% on 'remigration'; now leading opposition party.
Stocker ÖVP–SPÖ–NEOS coalition sworn in 3 Mar 2025 (first tripartite govt since 1949); stricter asylum agreed.
Coalition tightening: B1→B2 German for citizenship, Jan 2026 integration sanctions, family-reunion pause until 30 Sep 2026.
Migration and border control remain dominant political themes.

links.title: The Local – Immigration changes in Austria 2026 and beyond

tax.summary: Heavy burden: progressive income tax to 55% plus ~18% employee social contributions; ~37-39% combined load.
tax.reasoning: Austria's 2026 income tax is progressive from 20% (above ~€12,816) to 55% (above €1m), with the tax-free threshold raised to ~€13,541, and employees pay ~18% social contributions on top (pension 10.25%, health 3.87%, unemployment 3%). Combined with employer contributions the total load is ~37-39%; the flat 6% tax on 13th/14th salaries softens it slightly, but two well-paid engineers keep materially less take-home than in lower-tax destinations.
evidence: 2026 brackets: 0% to €13,541, then 20/30/40/48/50% up to €1m, 55% above; brackets indexed +1.73% for 2026.
Employee social contributions ~18% of gross; combined with employer ~21% gives ~39% total.
13th/14th salary (jahressechstel) taxed at flat 6%, first €620 of each tax-free.
Max monthly contribution base €6,450 (2026).

muslim-diaspora.summary: Islam legally recognised since 1912; ~8.3% Muslim, 200+ mosques/prayer rooms and abundant halal in Vienna; but rising political hostility.
muslim-diaspora.reasoning: Vienna has a large, established Muslim infrastructure (200+ mosques/prayer rooms incl. the Vienna Islamic Centre, abundant halal food) with Islam legally recognised since 1912 and Muslims ~8.3% nationally (much higher in Vienna), plus an organised Bangladeshi community (embassy, BCCA cricket club). The base is mainly Turkish/Bosnian rather than South Asian, and rising FPÖ-driven anti-Muslim sentiment plus tightening integration rules temper an otherwise strong community fit.
evidence: Islam recognised by law since 1912; ~8.3% of Austria's population Muslim (Statistik Austria 2021), higher in Vienna.
Vienna has 200+ mosques/prayer rooms (incl. Vienna Islamic Centre) and a well-established halal food scene.
Bangladeshi presence: Embassy/Permanent Mission in Vienna and Bangladesh Cricket Club Austria (BCCA).
Muslim base mainly Turkish/Bosnian; FPÖ-driven anti-immigrant sentiment and 2026 integration sanctions are a downward pull.

links.title: Embassy & Permanent Mission of Bangladesh, Vienna
